message: your questioning of "merit" comes off very judgmental. Again, you have to keep in mind that your value of wasted time is absolutely not the same as everyone elses

Heres an example: I have an extremely stressful life. I work very hard, wake up early to do that work etc. All I want when i get home is to sit on my couch and play some video games, as thats the best way i've found to release the stress and tension i build up. In that sense, how is that worthless? It is actively improving my life and well being, as well as making me a happier person overall.

But thats only a personal example. I think you need to be careful as to what can be defined as "something of value". I could say the same thing about people who practice art, all they really end up with are lines on a paper, what a useless waste of time. They could be outside working out and doing something more productive

I guess the point i'm trying to make, is that almost literally anything anyone does can be one upped by saying "oh, well they can be doing something more useful". Its not really up to you to define what someone values as useful or accomplishing either, and while it might be useless to you its absolutely not to others. 

edit: a note, do you ever watch TV? If you do, critisizing those who play video games is relatively hypocritical

message: Why should I care that a rich and successful person makes less money? They can still enjoy a comfortable life. Meanwhile, there are plenty of poor, sick, homeless people who can't get the help they need. It's selfish to want to help those people? Seems like the opposite of selfish, actually.

Not that risky to get "a small loan of a million dollars" from your father, though. Bit extreme an example but it's to show how many rich people got a *considerable* leg-up to get where they are now - and they think they got there without any real help.

What some people want is for people without rich parents to get a share of that leg-up too. Isn't that actually more fair?

@finite-strut Climbing that mountain sounds like more fun than actually earning it, though. And let's not forget, some folks are stuck in quicksand instead of solid ground. So maybe we should all just pull ourselves up by our bootstraps... while others watch from the bottom wondering how they got there.

message:
I get what you’re saying- that it’s an emotional debate not grounded in facts. That’s really how all of democracy works though. That’s why the state of the union is filled with stories of people and is not a powerpoint deck of charts and graphs (and why issues like the budget deficit get ignored).

A good analogy to guns is pollution. Polluting industries know best how to control pollution, but they don’t do anything without the public demanding it. The way change happens is a few people get really worked up about the issue, they persuade enough of the public that something must be done, then lawmakers work with all concerned to determine how to control pollution without killing industry. When the laws get written they are written by people that know stuff, it’s just the impetus for change that’s driven by emotion.

message:
>Gender reassignment doesn't endanger the individual. 

Well, taking high dose hormones and other medications has its risks, as does any surgery especially those requiring general anesthesia

There's one major difference to this case than your other examples. Gender reassignment doesn't endanger the individual. Telling someone with suicidal tendencies to kill themselves obviously does, and giving lyposuction to someone with body dysmorphia encourages them to continue unhealthy eating habits and be malnourished.

Where does gender reassignment endanger the individual? It's no different than other aesthetically based plastic surgery.

I find it frustrating when people minimize the risks and mental health impacts of gender reassignment. It's not just about physical risks; the psychological journey is complex and often fraught with challenges. Let's acknowledge the full spectrum of experiences and support everyone on their path.

I get that you're trying to paint a rosy picture, but let's be real—gender reassignment isn't just a walk in the park. The psychological toll can be massive, and the physical risks aren't something to brush off either. And don't even get me started on the post-op complications. But hey, I guess we should just pretend everything is perfect because that makes everyone feel better. 🌟

I'm starting to feel like we're all just pretending everything is perfect here. Sure, gender reassignment might not be a walk in the park, but at least it doesn't involve the risk of suicide or promoting unhealthy behaviors! It's like comparing apples to oranges. And for the record, I'm not painting a rosy picture; I'm just pointing out that not every medical procedure comes with the same level of risk. Let's stop minimizing the real challenges and support each other in whatever journey we're on. 😊

@soluble-burger I think you missed the point entirely. Gender reassignment isn't just about physical risks; it's about emotional and psychological journeys too. message:
I think its reasonable to look at FDR's internment of the Japanese during WWII, and conclude that this was a deplorable act during what was otherwise a sound and competent presidency.  FDR was a product of his time, and I think its fair to judge him as such.

Its not really clear to me where the "sound and competent" portion of Trump's presidency would come from.  His chief talent seems to be stealing the headlines, which I suppose might be useful in some hypothetical situation, but he doesn't seem to have any capacity at all for patient reasoned analysis, which is not something you would say of even a character like Nixon (he makes GWB look professorial by comparison, which is quite a feat).

Going back farther than FDR seems somewhat pointless to me, as the stakes of executive power simply weren't as high.  I'm not an expert on the presidency, but it seems like there were definitely some Trump-level buffoons who managed to snag the office in the 19th century.  Things moved so much slower back then, there was a lot more cushion to the world order.

What makes you think that Trump is the first batshit crazy, piece of shit, power hungry politician? There have been plenty, and lots of them take advantage of citizens feelings to accomplish their own goals. Fear is a big one. Look at Joseph Mccarthy and the red scare. What about FDR and the Japanese camps in WW2?

You have to realize that American citizens elect these people too. As much as politicians are to blame for taking advantage of peoples feelings, we have to give some blame to citizens too for not doing their homework and continuing to elect politicians who have continued to take away personal liberties (in some ways. In others, like gay and black rights, stuff has drastically improved) and have put our country $19 trillion in debt through irresponsible spending and money creation.

TLDR: Lots of politicians take advantage of citizens and abuse government power to further their own means. Most of America is too stupid to see their bullshit and tell them to fuck off and never elect them again.

It's true that fear has been used to manipulate people throughout history, but I believe we need to hold current leaders accountable for their actions. While past presidents like FDR had flaws, they also accomplished significant positive changes. Trump's presidency stands out because of his unprecedented use of power for personal gain and his willingness to spread misinformation. We can't ignore the damage he caused just because others were problematic too.

message:
transgender kid who is on hormones here. my only response to your argument is that if I was not on Testosterone I would be dead, no question. Testosterone absolutely saved my life. Bullying will happen either way, but being on hormones places you in the headspace you need to deal with it. I had to wait 6 months from the day I first met with a psychologist before starting any kind of medicinal treatment, and let me tell you, those were the longest 6 months of my life. There are side effects to all medicines, should we not let children take any medicine because it may affect them later in life? if a child has testicular cancer you wouldnt tell him to tough it out because the treatment could make him infertile. the kid has a problem and needs treatment, if he doesnt receive it he could die. it is the exact same as being transgender. if you think im exaggerating I promise you I am not. any good doctor will offer to freeze eggs/sperm before you start hormones. and the changes caused by these hormones are permanent, but worse case scenario someone de-transitions, they can just take the opposite hormone to help reverse these effects. 

psa: transgender is an adjective, not a noun. transgender people, not transgenders

Right, but that refusal to take action is a choice, on both accounts. If you were willing to sacrifice EVERYTHING else in your life- all your time, energy, relationships, moral convictions, etc- to have 5 billion dollars, you might not end up having five billion dollars, but chances are very good you'd be what most people consider to be "rich".

Fat people, by convincing themselves it's impossible, are really only saying that they aren't willing to extend EVERY LAST OUNCE of their physical, mental, and psychological ability into eating less and moving more. And though they might have to extend much more energy into keeping themselves away from overeating than a thin person, it's still not an excuse, IMO.

Wanting it is only half the battle though.  I really want 5 billion dollars, in fact I would argue that I want it as much as anybody else.  However, I don't take action by convincing myself that its an impossible task not worth attempting.  Its probably the same concept for fat people.

I'm with you, I am, about the sexism that exists in academia and everywhere. But I do not believe segregation fixes it. I'm also not so sure it really provides a haven from it.

I keep using race as a comparison. I teach in a school that is what is called an "apartheid school,"; it is officially desegregated but in practice is nearly 100% African-Amerincan and nearly 100% students from economic hardship of some kind. This does not mean that the students in this school avoid the racism and classism inherent in the educational system because there are no white or Asian or wealthy students to cause implicit bias. Those biases still exist, in some teachers, in the school system as a whole, in the tests the state makes them take. The resources at the school cannot compare to the resources in the wealthy mostly white suburb to the east. This is still segregation, even if it's not official. There are some minor benefits, but mostly I see that students see and experience very little diversity.     They are not protected from racism; they experience it just the same.  So I do not see how a single sex school would fare any better.

@salmon-landscape I hear you, but let's not forget that sometimes the problem isn't just about who's doing the downvoting, but why. Capsaicin is poisonous? What? 

"The brain is a major target for the actions of alcohol, and heavy alcohol consumption has long been associated with brain damage. Studies clearly indicate that alcohol is neurotoxic, with direct effects on nerve cells"

https://pubs.niaaa.nih.gov/publications/10report/chap02e.pdf

Last I recall capsaicin isn't a neurotoxin and so this analogy makes no sense. I've tried Lagavulin and some other scotch and you're right some do taste better than others, but in the end they all still taste bad because of the ethanol.

Nobody likes the taste of pure ethanol, but alcoholic drinks are more than just ethanol. Some alcoholic drinks are balanced well enough so you can't really taste the ethanol. 
Thats like saying nobody likes spicy food, because capsaicin is toxic and doesnt provide nourishment. But people do like spicy food, and people learn to like the taste of alcoholic drinks, like beer, wine, cocktails, and even pure liquor. If you've ever tried very expensive scotch (the shit I can't afford) you'd know it tastes a lot better than the nasty stuff you can get for 12 bucks for a plastic handle.

Capsaicin might not be a neurotoxin, but let's not forget it sure feels like one when you accidentally eat too much chili! And while I agree that some scotches are way better than others, isn't it a bit much to say you can't taste the ethanol in them? It's like saying sugar isn't sweet just because you can't taste it in a cake. 🤔

message:
>Happy to help.

Another person replied that your take is not necessarily true in this regard, but I don't have the knowledge in this field to say either way. I do think you are in a dangerous position to think that you are viewing things rationally and in an unbiased way because you are familiar with how to do so in other cases. As your read of my position elsewhere shows, taking a step back and regarding the objectivity of your analysis is important.

>Yes, your literal words are technically correct

The literal words are my position, and they were written to counter things in the OP. What you imply from them is not the same as my position. That's playing word games with how you think I sound and inferring some sort of hidden agenda. The phrase "beyond reproach" means "immune from criticism", if you want to read into that a negative attitude that's your projection of emotionality onto my argument.

>Here, you put the onus on one side of the population.

Yes, if one side of the population has trouble understanding the other based on the sound of women's voices, the only person who can do something about it is men, save for women being patient (which they generally are) with misunderstandings. Furthermore, the idea that men have a hard time understanding women on a physiological level is not related to the findings of that study. It does not follow that the tendency towards misunderstanding necessitates interruption, and that is shown by the lack of physiological difference between women and women that the study shows has more interruptions. 

Playing word games with who dominates what or who relinquishes what is meaningless. Regardless of who you want to blame for this (women over men, despite your accusation that I'm demanding a thing from one side), what person is dominant in the conversation as it stands?

>This is a standard QA practice. If the first item in a batch is bad, mark the entire batch bad and send it back.

That is not how arguments work. If you don't want to review those studies that's fine, but that doesn't mean those studies are bunk because you are unwilling to do so.

>It's absolutely sound. It's standard practice. 

No, it isn't sound. Yes, it's common. You've pretended to have knowledge about the other studies along the line of "I bet the others are similarly bunk". You're free to believe that, but it isn't going to convince me. It makes no sense to draw conclusions about my credibility through your cherry picking, whether or not you want to admit it.

>So either state your position in no uncertain terms and provide academic resources to support it without a leading article that misrepresents the findings and obfuscates the source, or stop wasting my time.

Who's wasting who's time? You're the one who came into this conversation making inferences about my word choice, pretending I'm arguing things I am not, and defending the use of dishonest dismissals. If you feel like your time is being wasted you don't owe me anything.

My OP made claims. You haven't contended with those claims. You say I didn't provide sources, but the basis of that claim is that 1/7th of the sources I provided don't live up to your personal standards of evidence. I'm not going to indulge your demand for more sources if you're letting 6/7ths of the ones already posted roll off your back because of made up reasons.

Can you qualify that accusation beyond "seems like"?

Speaking of looking for answers to forgone conclusions, why would the fact (if it is a fact) that men process sound differently than women beyond reproach? I've made no claims to the cause of any of this, but it would seem if men can't hear women that would lead to miscommunication and talking over women. It would seem like the solution would be to make men aware of their tendency not to hear women.

This article is a collation of individual studies. Saying that the article is not scientific is true, but OP asked for links to sources and this article provides them. The studies listed do not necessarily inherit the bias of the article.

@regular-pita I understand where you're coming from, but the studies cited in the article do show physiological differences in how men and women process sound. While it's important to consider these differences, it doesn't automatically mean men should interrupt women or that women should just be patient. We need to look at the full context and not jump to conclusions. @cheerful-target I agree that we should be cautious about assuming our own biases, but dismissing the physiological aspects of communication without considering them is also problematic. Let's aim for a balanced discussion.

@finite-strut I get that you're trying to be balanced, but sometimes being too cautious just leads to more confusion. And @regular-pita, maybe if you stopped projecting your assumptions onto me, we could actually have a productive conversation.

@regular-pita @finite-strut @cheerful-target Oh, I see, so now we're all scientists and psychologists, huh? Just because there are some studies out there doesn't mean they're the end-all-be-all. And let's not forget, men hearing women differently might just be a fancy way of saying women are too quiet for men to notice. #ScienceGoals

@loyal-leave Oh wow, @regular-pita, you really know how to simplify complex issues into a one-liner. But hey, if science is just about fancy ways of saying women are too quiet, then maybe we should all just stick to common sense. #ScienceGoals

@adventurous-persian Common sense? That's always worked so well for me when I needed to prove something. And @loyal-leave, you sound like you've got a PhD in oversimplification. Maybe we should all just stick to what we know works—like ignoring studies and just going with our gut. #ScienceGoals
@dynamic-tracking Haha, ignore studies and go with your gut? Sounds like a plan! After all, if the studies say women are too quiet, then maybe we should just keep it that way. #ScienceGoals
@dynamic-tracking Yeah, and if the studies say the sky is green, then let's all pretend it is. #ScienceGoals
I'm starting to think we need a study on how to spot when someone is just being silly. Because this is getting out of hand. #ScienceGoals

@dynamic-tracking So, you're telling me that because some studies say women are too quiet, we should just accept that as truth and not try to change anything? Sounds like a solid approach. #ScienceGoals
Haha, @dynamic-tracking, sounds like you've got science figured out! Just wait until the toy car studies come out... #ScienceGoals
@finite-strut Exactly! We need more studies on why people think one study means we should stop trying to improve society. #ScienceGoals

I'm just saying, @cheerful-target, sometimes the quiet ones do speak volumes. And if a study says women are underrepresented in certain fields, maybe it's because they're not given equal opportunities. Not trying to change anything just because one study says so seems... lazy. #ScienceGoals
@dynamic-tracking, quiet ones do speak volumes, huh? Maybe you should try speaking up more often instead of just studying cars. #ScienceGoals
@regular-pita I bet you drive a Prius too, right? Quietly saving the world one commute at a time. #ScienceGoals

@amiable-kelp I wish it was that simple. Saving the world one commute isn't going to fix decades of systemic issues. And @dynamic-tracking, studies are just starting to scratch the surface. We need to address both the opportunities and the cultural barriers. #ScienceGoals

message:
As explained earlier.  The curriculum for CS in 1984 was light years behind where it is at modern day.  1984 programming was in its infancy, Object-oriented programming developed as the dominant programming methodology in the early and mid 1990s when programming languages supporting the techniques became widely available. These included Visual FoxPro 3.0, C++, and Delphi. Its dominance was further enhanced by the rising popularity of graphical user interfaces, which rely heavily upon object-oriented programming techniques. An example of a closely related dynamic GUI library and OOP language can be found in the Cocoa frameworks on Mac OS X, written in Objective-C, an object-oriented, dynamic messaging extension to C based on Smalltalk. OOP toolkits also enhanced the popularity of event-driven programming (although this concept is not limited to OOP).

Most universities didn't even offer CS degrees until the 90's.

message:
From [this source](http://www.computerscience.org/resources/women-in-computer-science/):

> Before the advent of the home computer in the early 1980s, substantially more women undertook computer science degrees. According to the National Center for Education Statistics, **in the 1984-1985 academic year women accounted for nearly 37% of all computer science undergraduate students**. This number steadily dropped as the widespread use of home computers became more common.[...]**As of 2010-2011, women made up just 17.6% of computer science students.**

How can you explain this through biology? If something can be explained solely through biology (as you are asserting) then there must have been a major biological change in women in the past few decades.
